topics.name: AI-Powered Solopreneurship & Agentic Automation
details: **What is happening:** AI, particularly 'agentic AI,' is transforming how solopreneurs operate, allowing them to automate entire workflows, administrative tasks, content creation, and even customer service, effectively giving one person the output of a small team.

**Why it matters:** This trend directly addresses the core pain points of time scarcity and workload for solopreneurs, enabling them to work less while earning more by leveraging technology to scale their impact and reclaim hours previously spent on repetitive tasks.

topics.name: Anti-Hustle & Lifestyle-First Business
details: **What is happening:** There's a significant cultural shift, particularly driven by Gen Z, rejecting traditional hustle culture in favor of sustainable work practices, prioritizing mental health, setting boundaries, and designing businesses around personal well-being rather than relentless output.

**Why it matters:** FieldWork's 'Live Offline' ethos directly aligns with this desire for a business that supports a fulfilling life, offering strategies to achieve financial success without sacrificing personal time, health, or relationships.

topics.name: Solopreneur Economy Boom & Escaping 9-to-5
details: **What is happening:** A record number of individuals are choosing solopreneurship, driven by a desire to escape the traditional 9-to-5, gain control over their work and income, and build businesses that offer greater flexibility and autonomy.

**Why it matters:** This trend signifies a large and growing audience actively seeking alternative paths to work, making FieldWork's promise of 'escaping the 9-to-5 grind' highly relevant and timely for aspiring and current solopreneurs.

topics.name: Reclaiming Time & Sustainable Work-Life Integration
details: **What is happening:** Solopreneurs are actively seeking strategies and tools to reclaim their time, establish clear boundaries, and build businesses that support a sustainable work-life integration rather than leading to burnout.

**Why it matters:** FieldWork directly addresses the goal of 'reclaiming their time' and 'working less while earning more' by providing frameworks and automation strategies that enable founders to achieve greater efficiency and create a business that serves their life.

topics.name: Digital Detox & Intentional Offline Living
details: **What is happening:** Consumers and solopreneurs are increasingly seeking to reduce screen time and engage in intentional digital detox to combat digital fatigue, improve mental well-being, and foster deeper real-world connections.

**Why it matters:** This trend is a direct call to action for FieldWork's 'Live Offline' component, providing a strong hook for content that helps solopreneurs create boundaries with technology and prioritize real-world experiences and relationships.

topics.name: Building Lean, Automated One-Person Companies
details: **What is happening:** Solopreneurs are leveraging modular tech stacks, AI, and automation to build lean, resilient businesses with minimal overhead, allowing them to scale revenue and impact without scaling complexity or needing to hire employees.

**Why it matters:** FieldWork can provide the blueprints and strategies for building these efficient, automated systems, demonstrating how solopreneurs can achieve significant business growth and financial freedom as a 'one-person company'.

topics.name: Micro-Offers & Productized Knowledge
details: **What is happening:** Solopreneurs are finding success by creating and selling 'micro-offers' or productized services—small, highly specific digital products, templates, or short sprints—that deliver clear outcomes quickly, allowing them to diversify income streams without increasing their workload.

**Why it matters:** FieldWork can teach solopreneurs how to identify their expertise, package it into scalable digital products, and leverage automation to sell these micro-offers, directly contributing to the goal of 'earning more while working less'.

topics.name: Time & Location Freedom
details: **What is happening:** Solopreneurs are increasingly prioritizing the ability to work from anywhere, seeking geographical and time freedom to travel, live abroad, or simply have more flexibility in their daily lives, often enabled by remote-first business models.

**Why it matters:** This aligns perfectly with FieldWork's 'Build Online. Live Offline.' mantra, offering solutions for solopreneurs to design businesses that support a nomadic or location-independent lifestyle, allowing them to spend more time outside and offline.

posts.hook: Nothing better than catching giant tuna with the boys on some deserted islands…
posts.creator: wadepapenfus
posts.headline: This Is Why We Build
posts.angle: Instead of fishing, Christian's version shows the tangible result of building an automated business: offline moments like hiking, working in a shop, or sitting around a campfire. The reel celebrates the 'Live Offline' part of his mantra, the ultimate payoff for his audience.
hooks: Build the machine. Then leave the machine.
For the mountains, the river, the time in between.
Code on the screen. Dirt on the hands.
Build for your freedom. Not for the demands.
Systems run the business. So you can run the trails.
talking_points: Everyone is talking about how to build an online business. They're focused on funnels, ads, and scaling to the moon.
But they almost never talk about the entire point of doing it in the first place.
The goal isn't to get better at living online. It's to build something that buys back your time to live offline.
It's about building systems. Installing automations. Creating a business that works without you chained to a desk.
A tool waits for you to use it. A system runs without you.
You build these engines so you can have more of this. More time in the field. More time with your hands dirty on projects you actually love, with the people you want to be with.
You build the machine so you can leave the machine.
That's the entire philosophy behind FieldWork. We build online so we can live offline.
If you want to learn how we do it, just comment 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details.
posts.body_copy: Everyone is talking about how to build an online business. They're focused on funnels, ads, and scaling to the moon. But they almost never talk about the entire point. The goal isn't to get better at living online. It's to build something that buys back your time to live offline. It's about building systems. Installing automations. Creating a business that works without you chained to a desk. A tool waits for you to use it. A system runs without you. You build these engines so you can have more of this. More time in the field. More time with your hands dirty on projects you actually love, with the people you want to be with. You build the machine so you can leave the machine. That's the entire philosophy behind FieldWork. We build online so we can live offline. If you want to learn how we do it, just comment 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details.
script: Hook: Everyone is focused on the 'how' of online business, not the 'why'.
Problem: The goal isn't to live online; it's to earn your time back offline.
Solution: Build systems and automations that run without you.
Payoff: This creates freedom for tangible, offline experiences.
Philosophy: You build the machine so you can leave the machine.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' to learn how.
guidance: Shoot a simple, direct-to-camera talking head for the main script, preferably in an outdoor or workshop setting.
Splice in high-quality B-roll of you and friends hiking, working on a van, around a campfire, or any tangible 'offline' activity.
Use a short, powerful on-screen text hook like 'Build the machine. Then leave the machine.' over the opening B-roll shot.
The final shot should be your talking head, with a simple text overlay on the bottom third: 'Comment: FieldWork'.

posts.hook: My 13 week old golden retriever named froth now shreds after her first ever surf
posts.creator: wadepapenfus
posts.headline: Training My AI Employee
posts.angle: Frame this as a raw, behind-the-scenes look at the 'unsexy' work of building an automated system. Show the frustrating failures and the satisfying moment it finally works, connecting that effort to the 'Live Offline' payoff.
hooks: This is what building a real system looks like.
I’m training an AI to run my business for me.
It never works perfectly the first time.
Day one of teaching my business to run itself.
Forget the highlight reel. This is the real work.
talking_points: Alright, let's see if this works.
Okay, run the lead qualification sequence.
No. That's not the right tone at all. Too corporate.
And the follow up logic is broken. Stop.
Okay. Let's tweak the core prompt. Add my voice profile.
And we'll simplify the conditional logic here.
Alright. Run it again.
There it is. Perfect. In my voice, 24/7.
This is the work. It's not a single prompt. You test, it breaks, you fix it, you test again.
But once it's trained, it works for you while you're out in the field. That's the entire point of FieldWork.
If you want to learn how to build and install systems like this, comment the word 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details.
posts.body_copy: This is what it actually looks like to build a system. It never works on the first try. Okay, run the new lead sequence. Nope. Stop. The tone is wrong, too corporate. The follow up logic is broken. Okay. Let's go back in. We'll tweak the main prompt, be more specific about the voice. And simplify the logic right here. Alright. Let's run it again. There it is. Perfect. That's the real work. It's not cinematic. You build it once, it breaks, and you fix it. But once that system is trained, it works for you while you're hiking, fishing, or with your family. It buys back your time so you can get back out in the field. That's the whole point. If you want to learn how I build these systems, just comment the word 'FieldWork' below.
script: Hook: Show the messy reality of building a business system.
Problem: The first attempt fails, producing the wrong output.
Process: Show the iterative process of tweaking prompts and logic.
Success: The second attempt works perfectly.
Payoff: This is the work that lets you live offline.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' to learn how.
guidance: Film screen recordings of an AI chatbot or automation tool (like Zapier/Make) first generating bad output, then good output after a change.
Use rapid, jarring cuts between the screen recordings and a close-up shot of your face reacting to the results.
Overlay simple, bold text for each beat: 'TEST', 'FAIL', 'FIX', 'SUCCESS', 'SYSTEM ONLINE'.
End the video with b-roll of you outdoors, away from the computer, to visually represent the 'Live Offline' payoff.
The audio should be your voice only, speaking the commands directly and clearly, with no background music.

posts.creator: wadepapenfus
posts.headline: The Day My Business Broke
posts.angle: Frame a common, soul-crushing manual task (like sending welcome DMs) as a catastrophic system failure. Then, reveal the solution is installing a real system that buys back your time, embodying the FieldWork philosophy.
hooks: The moment my entire lead-gen system failed.
My business almost died last Tuesday.
This is a six-figure business bottleneck.
How I lost 50% of my new leads in one day.
The most expensive mistake I ever made.
talking_points: My entire lead generation system broke last week.
And by 'system,' I mean me. On my phone. Manually sending a welcome DM to every single new follower.
I was in meetings, I was on a flight. I missed a few. Then a few more.
Just like that, dozens of warm leads went completely cold because the 'system' was busy.
A tool waits for you to use it. A real system runs without you.
So I stopped being the system. I installed an AI employee to do it instead.
Now, it engages every new follower in my voice, 24/7. It never gets tired. It never takes a day off.
It qualifies leads while I'm on a hike. It books calls while I sleep.
This is how you build a business that doesn't chain you to a desk. You build online so you can live offline.
If you want to install systems instead of being the system, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.
posts.body_copy: My entire lead generation system broke last week. And by 'system,' I mean me. On my phone. Manually sending a welcome DM to every single new follower. I was in meetings, I was on a flight. I missed a few. Then a few more. And just like that, dozens of warm leads went completely cold because the 'system' was busy. A tool waits for you to use it. A real system runs without you. So I stopped being the system. I installed an AI employee to do it instead. Now, it engages every new follower in my voice, 24/7. It never gets tired. It never takes a day off. It qualifies leads while I'm on a hike. It books calls while I sleep. This is how you build a business that doesn't chain you to a desk. You build online so you can live offline. If you want to install systems instead of being the system, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.
script: Hook with a catastrophic system failure.
Reveal the 'system' was just you doing manual DMs.
Explain the cost: dozens of lost leads.
Introduce the core principle: a system runs without you.
Present the solution: an AI employee that works 24/7.
Connect to the 'Build Online, Live Offline' ethos.
End with a clear, keyword-based Call to Action.
guidance: Opener: Quick shot of you looking stressed, swiping on your phone. Mimic the 'shaking' motion of the original reel but with a phone.
On-Screen Text: Use a bold, dramatic font for the hook ('My Entire Lead System Broke'). Use smaller, clean text for key concepts like 'AI Employee' and the CTA.
B-Roll: After revealing the solution, cut to b-roll of you outdoors, hiking, or not at a desk. Visually pay off the 'Live Offline' promise.
Pacing: Keep the video fast and under 45 seconds. Use quick cuts between you talking and the b-roll to maintain energy.
CTA: End with a full-screen text card: 'Comment FIELDWORK' for maximum clarity.

posts.creator: tomnoske
posts.headline: How I Really Rebuilt
posts.angle: Mirror the competitor's raw, confessional structure. Pose the common question about how you rebuilt after losing everything, but instead of landing on 'grit,' reveal the real answer was having systems that couldn't be stolen.
hooks: People ask how I rebuilt after losing everything. The honest answer isn't very motivational.
I wish I could tell you I rebuilt with pure grit. I didn't.
In 2018, I went for a hike in Alaska. When I came back, my window was smashed and everything I owned was gone.
Everyone wants the motivational story about hitting rock bottom. This isn't it.
The most common question I get is how I kept going. It wasn't hustle.
talking_points: People always ask me how I kept going after I lost everything in Alaska back in 2018.
They expect some story about grit. About how I just outworked the problem and willed my way back.
To be honest with you, I don't have that story. I don't think pure hustle would have saved me.
When I came back to my truck that day, the window was smashed. My cameras, my lenses, my hard drives with all my work... gone.
Everything I used to do my job was gone in an instant. But the business itself was still running.
My client intake forms were still working. My email sequences were still sending. My project management system was still assigning tasks.
They could steal my tools. But they couldn't steal my systems.
That's when I learned the real lesson. Hustle is fragile. It depends on you feeling motivated. It depends on your car window not getting smashed.
A system doesn't care how you feel. It just works. It runs while you sleep, or while you're on the side of a road in Alaska figuring things out.
So if you want to build something that lasts, don't just rely on grit. Build systems that can't be taken away from you. That's what we teach in FieldWork.
posts.body_copy: People ask how I kept going after losing everything in Alaska. They want the motivational story. The one about pure grit. I wish I had one for you, but I don't. In 2018 my truck window got smashed. Cameras, hard drives, client work... all gone. In that moment, hustle wouldn't have saved me. But something else did. They stole my gear, but they couldn't steal my systems. My automated client funnels were still running. My email sequences were still sending. The business kept breathing, even when I couldn't. That's the difference. Your motivation can disappear. Your grit can run out. But a system? A system runs no matter what. It works while you sleep, or while you're picking up the pieces. That's how you really rebuild. You don't just 'figure it out'. You build something that can't be broken in the first place.
script: Hook: How did you rebuild after losing everything?
The Anti-Answer: It wasn't grit or hustle.
The Story: My truck window was smashed in Alaska.
The Insight: They stole my gear, but not my systems.
The Philosophy: Systems are anti-fragile, hustle is not.
The Call to Action: Build systems that run without you.
guidance: Film this as a single, direct-to-camera take. Close, intimate framing to create a confessional, campfire story feel.
Cut to b-roll of the actual photos from the Alaska trip, especially the shot of the smashed truck window when you mention it.
Use bold, on-screen text for key phrases: 'It wasn't grit.', 'They couldn't steal my systems.', 'Systems > Hustle.'
End with a clear, simple on-screen text call to action: 'Build your system. Comment FIELDWORK'.

posts.hook: Where could you be six months from today?
posts.creator: tomnoske
posts.headline: The Six Month Engine
posts.angle: Frame the next six months not as a period of intense hustle, but as a focused sprint to build the systems that buy back your time forever. The goal isn't to work harder for six months, it's to build an engine so you can work less, permanently.
hooks: What if I told you you're only six months away from a business that runs itself?
Your next six months could buy you the next six years of freedom. Here's how.
What if for the next six months, your only job was to make yourself irrelevant to your business?
Six months. That's the gap between a business that owns you and a business you actually own.
Forget hustling for six months. Let's build an engine for six months instead.
talking_points: What if I told you you're only six months away? Not from some vague dream life. From a business that funds your life while you're actually out living it.
The income you want. The time you want. The freedom to be offline, with the people you love, doing the things you love.
What if I told you its success was practically guaranteed? There's just one condition.
For those six months, you have to stop chasing clients. You have to stop grinding. You have to do the one thing you know you should be doing.
You have to build the engine. The system.
Most people spend those six months hustling. More posts, more calls, more hours. It's a trap. It just builds a bigger cage.
But what if you spent that time installing one system? An AI that qualifies your leads. An automation that nurtures your audience. A machine that works while you're in the field.
You already know who you want to be. A founder who is free. Not a founder who is trapped.
Stop waiting for permission to build something that serves you. Instead of just being served by you.
We build those engines inside my program, FieldWork. If you want the blueprint for your next six months, comment the word FIELDWORK below. I'll send it over.
posts.body_copy: What if I told you you're only six months away? Not from some vague dream life. From a business that funds your life while you're out living it. The income you want. The time you want. The freedom to be offline. What if its success was practically guaranteed? There's one condition. For those six months, you stop chasing clients. You stop grinding. You build the engine. Once. Most people spend that time hustling. More posts, more calls, more hours. That's a trap that just builds a bigger cage. But what if you spent that time installing a system? An AI that qualifies leads while you sleep. An automation that nurtures your audience 24/7. You already know who you want to be. A founder who is free, not a founder who is trapped. We build these systems in my program, FieldWork. If you want the blueprint for your next six months, comment the word FIELDWORK. I'll send it over.
script: Hook with the six month promise.
Define the promise: a business that gives you time back.
State the condition: a focused sprint to build the engine.
Contrast the 'hustle trap' with building a system.
Give concrete examples of what the system does.
Call them to be the founder they want to be.
CTA: Comment FIELDWORK for the blueprint.
guidance: Shoot this as a direct to camera talking head. Keep it simple and grounded, maybe in your workshop or office space.
B-roll is key. Juxtapose shots of you building (laptop, whiteboard) with shots of you 'living offline' (hiking, with family, outdoors). Visually tell the 'Build Online. Live Offline.' story.
Use clean, bold on screen text for key phrases: 'Six Months', 'Build The Engine. Once.', 'Systems > Hustle'.
End with a clear, full screen text call to action: 'Comment: FIELDWORK'.

posts.creator: tomnoske
posts.headline: Your AI Tools Are Useless
posts.angle: Shift the audience's focus from collecting new AI tools to building automated systems around the ones they already have. The tool is a replaceable commodity; the system is the actual asset that buys back time.
hooks: If I can give you any advice on building a business, the specific AI tool you use almost doesn't matter.
It's 90% the system you build, not the AI tool you use.
You have enough AI tools. You don't have enough systems.
I could swap ChatGPT for Claude tomorrow and my business wouldn't miss a beat. Here's why.
Stop looking for the perfect AI tool. It doesn't exist. The perfect system does.
talking_points: If I can give you any advice on building an online business, it's this: the specific AI tool you use almost doesn't matter.
It is 90% the system you build around it. The automation. The triggers. The thing that runs without you.
The game I try to play is not about finding the newest tool. It's about building the simplest system. The tool is just a component I can plug in or out.
Most people just collect tools. They have a folder of 50 AI apps they never use. It's like having a garage full of car parts but no engine.
So here's a challenge for you. For the next 30 days, stop looking for new tools. Don't sign up for a single new thing.
Instead, pick one tool you already have and build one automated workflow around it. Just one.
Maybe it's a system that takes a new lead from your website and automatically sends them a welcome sequence in your voice.
Once you build that, you'll see. The tool itself is boring. The system running at 2 AM while you're asleep? That's the thing that actually buys back your time.
That's what we do in FieldWork. We don't collect tools. We build the systems that let you get back out in the field. If you want to learn how, comment 'System' below.
posts.body_copy: If I can give you any advice on building an online business, it's this: the specific AI tool you use almost doesn't matter. It is 90% the system you build around it. The automation, the triggers, the thing that runs without you. The game I play isn't finding the newest tool. It's building the simplest system. The tool is just a component I can plug in or out. So for you, I would just give yourself the next month and go, okay, I'm only allowed to build one automated system. Pick one tool you already have. Maybe it's a system that takes a new lead from your website and automatically sends them a welcome sequence in your voice. Build that first. Once you see a system running at 2 AM while you're asleep, you'll get it. That's the thing that actually buys back your time. That's what we do in FieldWork. We build those systems. If you want to learn how, comment 'System' below.
script: Hook: The specific AI tool you use doesn't matter.
Reframe: It's 90% system, 10% tool.
Problem: People collect tools, not build engines.
Challenge: For 30 days, build one automated system.
Benefit: The system running 24/7 is what buys back your time.
CTA: Comment 'System' to learn the FieldWork method.
guidance: Film as a direct-to-camera talking head, medium close-up, in a natural setting. Keep it grounded and conversational.
Use bold, clean on-screen text to emphasize key concepts: '90% System', '10% Tool', 'Stop Collecting Tools', 'Start Building Systems'.
Include 1-2 quick B-roll shots of a visual automation builder like Zapier or Make to show what a 'system' looks like.
End with the call to action 'Comment SYSTEM' as large text on the screen for the final 2-3 seconds.
Maintain a controlled, confident energy. The tone is a helpful secret, not a hyped-up sales pitch.

posts.hook: Comment SHOWBIZ to get the deets for the workshop.
posts.creator: takimoore
posts.headline: The Anti-Funnel System
posts.angle: Frame your lead generation as an automated 'Time Engine' that works 24/7, contrasting it with the complex, high-maintenance sales funnels people hate. Focus on the core FieldWork promise: buying back time.
hooks: My old agency did $220M for 117 brands. But this simple system saves me 15 hours a week.
I replaced my entire sales process with one automated system. Let me sketch it out for you.
This isn't a sales funnel. It's a system that qualifies founders for my program while I'm hiking.
My business enrolls new members every week. I haven't spoken to a single one of them beforehand.
Most funnels are built to sell. My system is built to sort. Here's the difference.
talking_points: My last company managed $220M for 117 different brands. It was built on complicated funnels. My new business runs on one simple system that saves me 15 hours a week. Let me show you how it works.
I call it the Time Engine. Every piece of content I post, like this one, has one job. Deliver value, and then point people to one specific asset.
For me, it's called the FieldWork Blueprint. I built it to answer one question: what's the one system I wish every founder understood before they tried to scale?
At the end of the blueprint, people who want to learn more are invited to comment a keyword on one of my posts.
That keyword triggers an AI employee. Not a person, not a VA. An automated system that qualifies them.
It figures out if they're a good fit or not. If they are, it sends them the details for FieldWork. If not, it politely lets them know.
This one system adds qualified founders to my pipeline every single day without me lifting a finger.
Here's why it works so well. Most funnels are built to sell. They use pressure and complexity. This is a system built to sort. It’s calm. It's clear. It respects people's time.
The best part? I set this up when my audience was a fraction of its size today. The blueprint was just a simple doc. You don't need a huge audience. You need one good system.
I'm teaching founders how to install this exact engine in FieldWork. If you want a copy of the blueprint that explains it, just comment FIELDWORK below and my system will send it to you.
posts.body_copy: My old agency did $220M for 117 brands. It was built on complicated funnels. My new business runs on one simple system, and it saves me about 15 hours a week. I call it the Time Engine. Let me sketch it out. Every piece of content I create has one job: deliver value and invite people to see a single asset. It's the FieldWork Blueprint. I built it to answer one question: what's the one thing I wish every founder saw before they tried to scale? At the end, if people want to learn more, they comment a specific word on a post. That comment triggers an AI employee that qualifies them automatically. If it's a good fit, the system sends them the details for FieldWork. This simple setup adds qualified founders to my pipeline every single day. Here's why it works. Most funnels are built to sell. This is a system built to sort. I set this up when my audience was tiny. You don't need a huge following. You just need one good system. If you want the blueprint, comment FIELDWORK below.
script: Hook: My $220M agency vs. my new 15-hour-a-week system.
Mechanism: Introducing 'The Time Engine'.
Process: All content points to one blueprint.
Automation: A keyword comment triggers an AI to sort leads.
Philosophy: A system sorts, a funnel sells.
Objection Handling: You don't need a big audience to start.
CTA: Comment 'FIELDWORK' to get the blueprint.
guidance: Film as a direct-to-camera talking head video in your studio or a quiet outdoor spot.
Use B-roll of you hiking or working outdoors when you mention 'living offline' or 'while I'm hiking'.
Show a quick screen recording or simple whiteboard sketch of the 'Content -> Blueprint -> AI' flow.
Use clean, bold on-screen text for key phrases: 'The Time Engine', 'System > Funnel', and the final CTA.
The final shot must have 'Comment: FIELDWORK' in large text on screen as you say the call to action.

posts.hook: I locked my 9-person sales team in a cupboard for 90 days.⁠
posts.creator: takimoore
posts.headline: I Fired Myself For 90 Days
posts.angle: Frame this as a real-world test of the 'Build Online. Live Offline' thesis. You deliberately stopped all manual, real-time work to prove that properly built AI and automation systems can run and grow a business without you chained to a desk.
hooks: I fired myself for 90 days as a crazy experiment.
For three months, I ghosted my own business. No posts. No DMs. No emails.
I stopped working IN my business for an entire quarter. Here's what happened to the revenue.
I paid myself to do nothing for 90 days to prove a point about automation.
I went completely offline for a quarter to see if my business would survive. It thrived.
talking_points: I fired myself for 90 days as part of a crazy experiment. I gave my AI systems the keys and just walked away.
The rules were simple. No logging into social media. No checking analytics. No sending a single 'just checking in' email. Nothing.
That was last year. It was an experiment to see if a business could truly run itself, or if it would burn to the ground without me.
Here's what actually happened. In the first 30 days, the systems didn't just keep the lights on. They brought in 212 new leads and closed over fifteen thousand dollars in sales.
By the end of the quarter, that number was over fifty thousand dollars. All while I was hiking in Montana, completely disconnected.
But the real win wasn't the money. It was the 15 hours a week I got back. Permanently.
So now you know it works. Let me show you how.
Step one is installing an AI employee. It handles your lead nurture in your voice, 24/7.
Step two is building a content engine. It turns one idea into a month of content that posts itself.
Step three is connecting them. You build a system that qualifies leads and books calls while you're in the field, living your life.
If you want the blueprint for how to build this, comment the word FIELDWORK below and I'll send it over.
posts.body_copy: I fired myself for 90 days as part of a crazy experiment. I gave my AI systems the keys and walked away. The rules were simple. No logging into social media. No checking analytics. No sending a single 'just checking in' email. Nothing. That was last year. It was an experiment to see if a business could truly run itself, or if it would just burn to the ground. Here's what happened. In the first 30 days, the systems didn't just keep the lights on. They brought in 212 new leads and closed over fifteen thousand dollars in sales. By the end of the quarter, that number was over fifty thousand. All while I was hiking in Montana, completely disconnected. But the real win wasn't the money. It was the 15 hours a week I got back, permanently. So now you know it works. Here's how. Step one is installing an AI employee to handle lead nurture. Step two is building a content engine that runs on its own. Step three is connecting them into a system that runs while you're in the field. If you want the blueprint for this, comment FIELDWORK below. I'll send it over.
script: Hook: The crazy 90 day experiment of 'firing myself'.
The Rules: No manual work, let the AI systems run everything.
The Results: Reveal escalating lead and revenue numbers.
The Real Win: The 15 hours per week permanently bought back.
The 'How': Tease the 3-step system of AI employees and content engines.
CTA: Comment FIELDWORK for the blueprint.
guidance: Film as a direct-to-camera talking head. Medium shot, in your studio or an outdoor setting. Maintain strong, confident eye contact throughout.
Use a bold, clean text hook for the first 3 seconds: 'I Fired Myself For 90 Days.'
Add simple text pop-ups to emphasize key numbers as you say them: '212 Leads', '$50,000', '15 Hours/Week'.

posts.hook: Part 3 of 5 — You do not have to force pay-in-full to build a great coaching bus
posts.creator: takimoore
posts.headline: Stop Building Yourself a Job
posts.angle: Apply the 'They say X, but I do Y' structure to the hustle culture vs. systems debate. Frame the common advice to 'be on 24/7' as a trap, and position the FieldWork philosophy of building automated systems as the true path to freedom.
hooks: They say to grow your business, you have to be 'on' 24/7. You can't tell me what to do.
Everyone tells you to hustle. To answer every DM. To never log off. That's a trap.
Stop trading your time for money. There's a better way.
The goal isn't to build a business that needs you. It's to build one that runs without you.
They tell you to grind. I tell you to build a system. Here's the difference.
talking_points: They say to grow your business, you have to be 'on' 24/7. You have to answer every DM, post ten times a day, and never log off.
I get it. When you're starting out, your energy is the only thing moving it forward. You are the business.
But the mistake is thinking the goal is to build a business that needs you. For me, the goal is to build a business that buys back my time.
People in my world build these engines and then go live their lives. I've got clients who work 15 hours a week and make more than they did working 60.
I used to do the manual thing. Every lead was a DM I had to answer. Every follow up was a calendar reminder. It was just a job I couldn't quit.
So now we just build it right the first time. We install AI employees and automation that handle 90 percent of the work.
The system runs whether I'm at my desk or on a mountain. It doesn't care. It just works.
So if someone tells you that you have to hustle harder, that you need to be available 24/7 to succeed... remember it's your business. You can build it however you want.
There are hundreds of ways to grow. You just have to find the one that gives you the life you actually want.
If you want to build a business that sets you free instead of one that locks you up, comment 'FieldWork' below and I'll show you how we do it.
posts.body_copy: They say to grow your business, you have to be 'on' 24/7. You have to answer every DM and never log off. I get it. When you're starting, your energy is the only thing moving it forward. You are the business. But the mistake is thinking the goal is to build a business that needs you. For me, the goal is to build a business that runs without me. I used to do the manual thing. Every lead was a DM I had to answer. Every follow up was a calendar reminder. It was a job I couldn't quit. So now, we just build it right the first time. We install AI employees and automation that handle 90 percent of the work. The system runs whether I'm at my desk or on a mountain. It doesn't care. It just works. So if someone tells you that you have to hustle harder to succeed, remember it's your business. You can build it however you want. If you want to build a business that sets you free, not one that locks you up, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll show you how.
script: Hook: Challenge the 'always on' hustle culture.
Acknowledge: It makes sense at first, but it's a trap.
Reframe: The goal is a business that runs WITHOUT you.
The Old Way: I used to build myself a high paying job.
The New Way: Now I build systems that run themselves.
Empower: You get to choose the business model you want.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' to learn how.
guidance: Shoot as a simple, direct to camera talking head. Location could be your office, but an outdoor or van setting would strongly reinforce the 'Live Offline' message.
Use B-roll that contrasts the two ideas: quick cuts of someone looking stressed at a laptop, contrasted with shots of you hiking, with family, or enjoying the outdoors.
Use bold, clean on-screen text for key phrases like: 'HUSTLE IS A TRAP', 'BUILD A BUSINESS THAT RUNS WITHOUT YOU', and 'SYSTEMS > HUSTLE'.
End with a clear, full-screen text card for the call to action: 'Comment "FieldWork"'.

ideas.title: Build the machine. Then leave the machine.
ideas.headline: This Is Why We Build
ideas.angle: Instead of fishing, Christian's version shows the tangible result of building an automated business: offline moments like hiking, working in a shop, or sitting around a campfire. The reel celebrates the 'Live Offline' part of his mantra, the ultimate payoff for his audience.
hooks: Build the machine. Then leave the machine.
For the mountains, the river, the time in between.
Code on the screen. Dirt on the hands.
Build for your freedom. Not for the demands.
Systems run the business. So you can run the trails.
talking_points: Everyone is talking about how to build an online business. They're focused on funnels, ads, and scaling to the moon.
But they almost never talk about the entire point of doing it in the first place.
The goal isn't to get better at living online. It's to build something that buys back your time to live offline.
It's about building systems. Installing automations. Creating a business that works without you chained to a desk.
A tool waits for you to use it. A system runs without you.
You build these engines so you can have more of this. More time in the field. More time with your hands dirty on projects you actually love, with the people you want to be with.
You build the machine so you can leave the machine.
That's the entire philosophy behind FieldWork. We build online so we can live offline.
If you want to learn how we do it, just comment 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details.

Creating a business that works without you chained to a desk. A tool waits for you to use it. A system runs without you. You build these engines so you can have more of this. More time in the field. More time with your hands dirty on projects you actually love, with the people you want to be with. You build the machine so you can leave the machine. That's the entire philosophy behind FieldWork. We build online so we can live offline. If you want to learn how we do it, just comment 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details.

Payoff: This creates freedom for tangible, offline experiences.
Philosophy: You build the machine so you can leave the machine.

Splice in high-quality B-roll of you and friends hiking, working on a van, around a campfire, or any tangible 'offline' activity.
Use a short, powerful on-screen text hook like 'Build the machine. Then leave the machine.' over the opening B-roll shot.
The final shot should be your talking head, with a simple text overlay on the bottom third: 'Comment: FieldWork'.

ideas.title: This is what building a real system looks like.
ideas.headline: Training My AI Employee
ideas.angle: Frame this as a raw, behind-the-scenes look at the 'unsexy' work of building an automated system. Show the frustrating failures and the satisfying moment it finally works, connecting that effort to the 'Live Offline' payoff.
hooks: This is what building a real system looks like.
I’m training an AI to run my business for me.
It never works perfectly the first time.
Day one of teaching my business to run itself.
Forget the highlight reel. This is the real work.
talking_points: Alright, let's see if this works.
Okay, run the lead qualification sequence.
No. That's not the right tone at all. Too corporate.
And the follow up logic is broken. Stop.
Okay. Let's tweak the core prompt. Add my voice profile.
And we'll simplify the conditional logic here.
Alright. Run it again.
There it is. Perfect. In my voice, 24/7.
This is the work. It's not a single prompt. You test, it breaks, you fix it, you test again.
But once it's trained, it works for you while you're out in the field. That's the entire point of FieldWork.
If you want to learn how to build and install systems like this, comment the word 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details.
ideas.body_copy: This is what it actually looks like to build a system. It never works on the first try. Okay, run the new lead sequence. Nope. Stop. The tone is wrong, too corporate. The follow up logic is broken. Okay. Let's go back in. We'll tweak the main prompt, be more specific about the voice. And simplify the logic right here. Alright. Let's run it again. There it is. Perfect. That's the real work. It's not cinematic. You build it once, it breaks, and you fix it. But once that system is trained, it works for you while you're hiking, fishing, or with your family. It buys back your time so you can get back out in the field. That's the whole point. If you want to learn how I build these systems, just comment the word 'FieldWork' below.
script: Hook: Show the messy reality of building a business system.
Problem: The first attempt fails, producing the wrong output.
Process: Show the iterative process of tweaking prompts and logic.
Success: The second attempt works perfectly.
Payoff: This is the work that lets you live offline.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' to learn how.
guidance: Film screen recordings of an AI chatbot or automation tool (like Zapier/Make) first generating bad output, then good output after a change.
Use rapid, jarring cuts between the screen recordings and a close-up shot of your face reacting to the results.
Overlay simple, bold text for each beat: 'TEST', 'FAIL', 'FIX', 'SUCCESS', 'SYSTEM ONLINE'.
End the video with b-roll of you outdoors, away from the computer, to visually represent the 'Live Offline' payoff.
The audio should be your voice only, speaking the commands directly and clearly, with no background music.

ideas.title: The moment my entire lead-gen system failed.

hooks: The moment my entire lead-gen system failed.
My business almost died last Tuesday.
This is a six-figure business bottleneck.
How I lost 50% of my new leads in one day.
The most expensive mistake I ever made.
talking_points: My entire lead generation system broke last week.
And by 'system,' I mean me. On my phone. Manually sending a welcome DM to every single new follower.
I was in meetings, I was on a flight. I missed a few. Then a few more.
Just like that, dozens of warm leads went completely cold because the 'system' was busy.
A tool waits for you to use it. A real system runs without you.
So I stopped being the system. I installed an AI employee to do it instead.
Now, it engages every new follower in my voice, 24/7. It never gets tired. It never takes a day off.
It qualifies leads while I'm on a hike. It books calls while I sleep.
This is how you build a business that doesn't chain you to a desk. You build online so you can live offline.
If you want to install systems instead of being the system, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.

ideas.title: People ask how I rebuilt after losing everything. The honest answer isn't very motivational.
ideas.headline: How I Really Rebuilt
ideas.angle: Mirror the competitor's raw, confessional structure. Pose the common question about how you rebuilt after losing everything, but instead of landing on 'grit,' reveal the real answer was having systems that couldn't be stolen.
hooks: People ask how I rebuilt after losing everything. The honest answer isn't very motivational.
I wish I could tell you I rebuilt with pure grit. I didn't.
In 2018, I went for a hike in Alaska. When I came back, my window was smashed and everything I owned was gone.
Everyone wants the motivational story about hitting rock bottom. This isn't it.
The most common question I get is how I kept going. It wasn't hustle.
talking_points: People always ask me how I kept going after I lost everything in Alaska back in 2018.
They expect some story about grit. About how I just outworked the problem and willed my way back.
To be honest with you, I don't have that story. I don't think pure hustle would have saved me.
When I came back to my truck that day, the window was smashed. My cameras, my lenses, my hard drives with all my work... gone.
Everything I used to do my job was gone in an instant. But the business itself was still running.
My client intake forms were still working. My email sequences were still sending. My project management system was still assigning tasks.
They could steal my tools. But they couldn't steal my systems.
That's when I learned the real lesson. Hustle is fragile. It depends on you feeling motivated. It depends on your car window not getting smashed.
A system doesn't care how you feel. It just works. It runs while you sleep, or while you're on the side of a road in Alaska figuring things out.
So if you want to build something that lasts, don't just rely on grit. Build systems that can't be taken away from you. That's what we teach in FieldWork.
ideas.body_copy: People ask how I kept going after losing everything in Alaska. They want the motivational story. The one about pure grit. I wish I had one for you, but I don't. In 2018 my truck window got smashed. Cameras, hard drives, client work... all gone. In that moment, hustle wouldn't have saved me. But something else did. They stole my gear, but they couldn't steal my systems. My automated client funnels were still running. My email sequences were still sending. The business kept breathing, even when I couldn't. That's the difference. Your motivation can disappear. Your grit can run out. But a system? A system runs no matter what. It works while you sleep, or while you're picking up the pieces. That's how you really rebuild. You don't just 'figure it out'. You build something that can't be broken in the first place.
script: Hook: How did you rebuild after losing everything?
The Anti-Answer: It wasn't grit or hustle.
The Story: My truck window was smashed in Alaska.
The Insight: They stole my gear, but not my systems.
The Philosophy: Systems are anti-fragile, hustle is not.
The Call to Action: Build systems that run without you.
guidance: Film this as a single, direct-to-camera take. Close, intimate framing to create a confessional, campfire story feel.
Cut to b-roll of the actual photos from the Alaska trip, especially the shot of the smashed truck window when you mention it.
Use bold, on-screen text for key phrases: 'It wasn't grit.', 'They couldn't steal my systems.', 'Systems > Hustle.'
End with a clear, simple on-screen text call to action: 'Build your system. Comment FIELDWORK'.

ideas.title: What if I told you you're only six months away from a business that runs itself?
ideas.headline: The Six Month Engine
ideas.angle: Frame the next six months not as a period of intense hustle, but as a focused sprint to build the systems that buy back your time forever. The goal isn't to work harder for six months, it's to build an engine so you can work less, permanently.
hooks: What if I told you you're only six months away from a business that runs itself?
Your next six months could buy you the next six years of freedom. Here's how.
What if for the next six months, your only job was to make yourself irrelevant to your business?
Six months. That's the gap between a business that owns you and a business you actually own.
Forget hustling for six months. Let's build an engine for six months instead.
talking_points: What if I told you you're only six months away? Not from some vague dream life. From a business that funds your life while you're actually out living it.
The income you want. The time you want. The freedom to be offline, with the people you love, doing the things you love.
What if I told you its success was practically guaranteed? There's just one condition.
For those six months, you have to stop chasing clients. You have to stop grinding. You have to do the one thing you know you should be doing.
You have to build the engine. The system.
Most people spend those six months hustling. More posts, more calls, more hours. It's a trap. It just builds a bigger cage.
But what if you spent that time installing one system? An AI that qualifies your leads. An automation that nurtures your audience. A machine that works while you're in the field.
You already know who you want to be. A founder who is free. Not a founder who is trapped.
Stop waiting for permission to build something that serves you. Instead of just being served by you.
We build those engines inside my program, FieldWork. If you want the blueprint for your next six months, comment the word FIELDWORK below. I'll send it over.

Contrast the 'hustle trap' with building a system.
Give concrete examples of what the system does.

ideas.title: If I can give you any advice on building a business, the specific AI tool you use almost doesn't matter.
ideas.headline: Your AI Tools Are Useless
ideas.angle: Shift the audience's focus from collecting new AI tools to building automated systems around the ones they already have. The tool is a replaceable commodity; the system is the actual asset that buys back time.
hooks: If I can give you any advice on building a business, the specific AI tool you use almost doesn't matter.
It's 90% the system you build, not the AI tool you use.
You have enough AI tools. You don't have enough systems.
I could swap ChatGPT for Claude tomorrow and my business wouldn't miss a beat. Here's why.
Stop looking for the perfect AI tool. It doesn't exist. The perfect system does.
talking_points: If I can give you any advice on building an online business, it's this: the specific AI tool you use almost doesn't matter.
It is 90% the system you build around it. The automation. The triggers. The thing that runs without you.
The game I try to play is not about finding the newest tool. It's about building the simplest system. The tool is just a component I can plug in or out.
Most people just collect tools. They have a folder of 50 AI apps they never use. It's like having a garage full of car parts but no engine.
So here's a challenge for you. For the next 30 days, stop looking for new tools. Don't sign up for a single new thing.
Instead, pick one tool you already have and build one automated workflow around it. Just one.
Maybe it's a system that takes a new lead from your website and automatically sends them a welcome sequence in your voice.
Once you build that, you'll see. The tool itself is boring. The system running at 2 AM while you're asleep? That's the thing that actually buys back your time.
That's what we do in FieldWork. We don't collect tools. We build the systems that let you get back out in the field. If you want to learn how, comment 'System' below.
ideas.body_copy: If I can give you any advice on building an online business, it's this: the specific AI tool you use almost doesn't matter. It is 90% the system you build around it. The automation, the triggers, the thing that runs without you. The game I play isn't finding the newest tool. It's building the simplest system. The tool is just a component I can plug in or out. So for you, I would just give yourself the next month and go, okay, I'm only allowed to build one automated system. Pick one tool you already have. Maybe it's a system that takes a new lead from your website and automatically sends them a welcome sequence in your voice. Build that first. Once you see a system running at 2 AM while you're asleep, you'll get it. That's the thing that actually buys back your time. That's what we do in FieldWork. We build those systems. If you want to learn how, comment 'System' below.
script: Hook: The specific AI tool you use doesn't matter.
Reframe: It's 90% system, 10% tool.
Problem: People collect tools, not build engines.
Challenge: For 30 days, build one automated system.
Benefit: The system running 24/7 is what buys back your time.
CTA: Comment 'System' to learn the FieldWork method.
guidance: Film as a direct-to-camera talking head, medium close-up, in a natural setting. Keep it grounded and conversational.
Use bold, clean on-screen text to emphasize key concepts: '90% System', '10% Tool', 'Stop Collecting Tools', 'Start Building Systems'.
Include 1-2 quick B-roll shots of a visual automation builder like Zapier or Make to show what a 'system' looks like.
End with the call to action 'Comment SYSTEM' as large text on the screen for the final 2-3 seconds.
Maintain a controlled, confident energy. The tone is a helpful secret, not a hyped-up sales pitch.

ideas.title: My old agency did $220M for 117 brands. But this simple system saves me 15 hours a week.
ideas.headline: The Anti-Funnel System
ideas.angle: Frame your lead generation as an automated 'Time Engine' that works 24/7, contrasting it with the complex, high-maintenance sales funnels people hate. Focus on the core FieldWork promise: buying back time.
hooks: My old agency did $220M for 117 brands. But this simple system saves me 15 hours a week.
I replaced my entire sales process with one automated system. Let me sketch it out for you.
This isn't a sales funnel. It's a system that qualifies founders for my program while I'm hiking.
My business enrolls new members every week. I haven't spoken to a single one of them beforehand.
Most funnels are built to sell. My system is built to sort. Here's the difference.
talking_points: My last company managed $220M for 117 different brands. It was built on complicated funnels. My new business runs on one simple system that saves me 15 hours a week. Let me show you how it works.
I call it the Time Engine. Every piece of content I post, like this one, has one job. Deliver value, and then point people to one specific asset.
For me, it's called the FieldWork Blueprint. I built it to answer one question: what's the one system I wish every founder understood before they tried to scale?
At the end of the blueprint, people who want to learn more are invited to comment a keyword on one of my posts.
That keyword triggers an AI employee. Not a person, not a VA. An automated system that qualifies them.
It figures out if they're a good fit or not. If they are, it sends them the details for FieldWork. If not, it politely lets them know.
This one system adds qualified founders to my pipeline every single day without me lifting a finger.
Here's why it works so well. Most funnels are built to sell. They use pressure and complexity. This is a system built to sort. It’s calm. It's clear. It respects people's time.
The best part? I set this up when my audience was a fraction of its size today. The blueprint was just a simple doc. You don't need a huge audience. You need one good system.
I'm teaching founders how to install this exact engine in FieldWork. If you want a copy of the blueprint that explains it, just comment FIELDWORK below and my system will send it to you.
ideas.body_copy: My old agency did $220M for 117 brands. It was built on complicated funnels. My new business runs on one simple system, and it saves me about 15 hours a week. I call it the Time Engine. Let me sketch it out. Every piece of content I create has one job: deliver value and invite people to see a single asset. It's the FieldWork Blueprint. I built it to answer one question: what's the one thing I wish every founder saw before they tried to scale? At the end, if people want to learn more, they comment a specific word on a post. That comment triggers an AI employee that qualifies them automatically. If it's a good fit, the system sends them the details for FieldWork. This simple setup adds qualified founders to my pipeline every single day. Here's why it works. Most funnels are built to sell. This is a system built to sort. I set this up when my audience was tiny. You don't need a huge following. You just need one good system. If you want the blueprint, comment FIELDWORK below.
script: Hook: My $220M agency vs. my new 15-hour-a-week system.
Mechanism: Introducing 'The Time Engine'.
Process: All content points to one blueprint.
Automation: A keyword comment triggers an AI to sort leads.
Philosophy: A system sorts, a funnel sells.
Objection Handling: You don't need a big audience to start.
CTA: Comment 'FIELDWORK' to get the blueprint.
guidance: Film as a direct-to-camera talking head video in your studio or a quiet outdoor spot.

ideas.title: We did $220M for 117 brands. And it taught me one thing: money is a terrible goal.
ideas.headline: It Was Never About The Money
ideas.angle: Mirror the original's structure. Start with your big agency number ($220M), then pivot to explain it was never the goal. The real goal was building systems that buy back your time, a lesson learned the hard way after losing everything in Alaska.
hooks: We did $220M for 117 brands. And it taught me one thing: money is a terrible goal.
My agency managed $220M in revenue. But my real goal was to never check email on a Saturday again.
I built a business that did $220M for its clients. Then I walked away to build one that gave me back my time.
The number was $220M. But the real win was building a business I didn't have to run from a desk.
After helping brands do $220M, I realized the real metric is how many hours you get to live offline.
talking_points: My old agency helped 117 brands generate over $220M. But it was never about the money.
It was always, always about the time. It was about freedom.
I didn't want to be a millionaire trapped in an office. I just wanted to build a business that didn't own me.
I wanted to be able to shut my laptop and actually be offline. In the mountains. With people I care about.
I learned this the hard way. In 2018, I came back from a hike in Alaska and my van window was smashed. Everything I owned was gone. My cameras, my hard drives, everything.
But they couldn't take the one thing that mattered. The system in my head.
That's the difference. A tool needs you to use it. A system runs without you.
Most people use their business as a reason not to live. You can build it to be the reason you finally can.
That's what we teach in FieldWork. How to build the systems that buy back your time.
If you want to learn how, just comment FIELDWORK below and I'll send you the details.
ideas.body_copy: My old agency helped brands do over $220M in revenue. But I'll tell you right now, it was never about the money. It was always about the time. I just wanted to build a business that didn't chain me to a desk, one that would let me shut my laptop and actually be offline. I learned this the hard way. In 2018, I came back from a hike in Alaska to find my van window smashed. Everything was gone. Cameras, hard drives, all of it. But they couldn't steal the most valuable thing I had: the system in my head. That's the difference. A tool needs you to work. A system works for you. You can build a business that becomes your prison, or you can build one that buys back your freedom. That's what we teach inside FieldWork. If you want to build online so you can live offline, comment FIELDWORK below. I'll send you the details.
script: Hook with the $220M agency number.
Pivot: It was never about the money, it was about time.
The catalyst: The Alaska story of losing everything tangible.
The insight: The only asset that mattered was the intangible system.
The lesson: A system runs without you, buying your freedom.
The offer: Comment FIELDWORK to learn how to build your own.
guidance: Film as a simple talking head, straight to camera. An outdoors or workshop background is ideal.
Use bold, clean on-screen text to emphasize key phrases: '$220M', 'Time, not money', 'A System Runs Without You'.
Quickly cut to 1-2 pieces of B-roll during the Alaska story: a photo of the van, a clip of you hiking, or just a scenic mountain shot.
Keep the energy grounded and confident, not hypey. Let the story provide the impact.
End with a clear, full-screen text card: 'Comment FIELDWORK'.

ideas.title: Most founders spend 30 days a month just feeding the machine.
ideas.headline: The Automated Work Week
ideas.angle: Contrast the feeling of being an employee in your own business with the freedom of having a system that works for you. Frame automation not just as a way to make more money, but as the engine that buys back your time to live offline.
hooks: Most founders spend 30 days a month just feeding the machine.
Your business should fund your life, not become your life.
There's a big difference between running a business and being run by one.
Stop trading your hours for dollars. It's a losing game.
Most businesses are built to consume your time. What if yours was built to buy it back?
talking_points: Most founders I know are basically just high paid employees in their own companies. They spend all month chasing work, servicing clients, and keeping the lights on. It's exhausting.
What if your business did the heavy lifting for you? What if you could build an engine that brought in clients for an entire month, in just a few days?
If that sounds like a better way to work, comment FIELDWORK below and I'll send you how it works.
But here's what I love most. When you automate the hard parts, you free up three weeks a month. Not to grind on the next launch. But to actually live.
To get back out in the field. To spend time with your family. To do the things your business is supposed to fund in the first place.
This isn't hustle. It's a system. The same kind of systems I've used to help over 100 brands generate $220M. They work without you.
I'm teaching a small group of founders how to install this exact engine in their business.
If you want to build online so you can live offline, comment FIELDWORK below. I'll send you the details.
ideas.body_copy: Most founders are employees in their own business. They spend 30 days a month chasing clients, servicing work, and just keeping the lights on. It's exhausting. What if your business did the heavy lifting for you? What if an automated system could bring in a whole month's worth of work in just a handful of days? If that sounds interesting, comment FIELDWORK below and I'll send you the details. But the best part isn't just compressing the work. It's about what you do with the other three weeks. You get your time back. Time to get out in the field, be with your family, and do the things your business is supposed to fund. This is not about hustle. It's about installing a system. The same systems thinking that helped my clients generate over $220M. I'm showing a small group how to build this engine inside FieldWork. If you want to build online so you can live offline, comment FIELDWORK and I'll send you the invite.
script: Problem: Founders are trapped working IN their business.
Aspiration: What if your business worked FOR you?
The Real Goal: Free up 3 weeks a month to LIVE.
The How: Install an automated system, don't just hustle.
Proof: The $220M systems framework.
Offer: Learn how inside the FieldWork program.
CTA: Comment FIELDWORK for the details.
guidance: Film as a direct to camera talking head. Keep it grounded and conversational, not hyped.
Use clean, bold text overlays for key phrases: '30 Days Chasing Clients', '3 Weeks Living Offline', and 'Build Online. Live Offline.'
Splice in 2-3 short b-roll clips of 'offline' life: hiking, fly fishing, time with family. This visually pays off the promise.
For the CTA, have a simple text overlay that says 'Comment: FIELDWORK' and physically point down towards the comments section.
